title: DORA Outsourcing Registers — Sub-Processor Notification Lag vs Contractual Notice Period
exclusions: Notifications within contractual notice period; entity name changes without processing changes; batch notification processing per contractual terms; general authorisation model objection windows.
evidence: Sub-processor notification evidence showing change dates, notification timestamps, acknowledgement records, due diligence completion, and register update confirmation.

compliance.assurance_rationale: DORA Art.5 — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that DORA Art.5 (ICT risk-management governance) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.3.32: DORA Outsourcing Registers — Sub-Processor Notification Lag vs Contractual Notice Period.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.3.32' running on sourcetype vendor:subproc_notice and supporting catalogue-defined data sources, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Log a test sub-processor notification arriving 75 days after the change (exceeding a 60-day contractual SLA); verify the lag detection identifies the late notification.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Log a test sub-processor notification arriving within 30 days of the change; verify no lag alert fires.

dataSources: `index=vendor` `sourcetype="vendor:subproc_notice"` (provider_id, subprocessor_name, effective_date, notice_received_at); `roi_subprocessor.csv` (provider_id, subprocessor_name, registered_at) (Splunkbase 1621 — Splunk CIM Add-on)
app: Splunk DB Connect, email ingestion (careful PII controls)
spl:
index=vendor sourcetype="vendor:subproc_notice" earliest=-400d
| eval notice=strptime(notice_received_at,"%Y-%m-%d"), eff=strptime(effective_date,"%Y-%m-%d")
| join type=left provider_id, subprocessor_name [
    | inputlookup roi_subprocessor.csv
    | eval reg=strptime(registered_at,"%Y-%m-%d")
    | fields provider_id, subprocessor_name, reg
  ]
| eval lag_days=round((reg-notice)/86400,1)
| where lag_days>14 OR isnull(reg)
| table provider_id, subprocessor_name, notice_received_at, registered_at, lag_days
description: Outsourcing arrangements require visibility into sub-processing changes. Comparing provider sub-processor announcement dates to your internal register update timestamps shows contractual notification periods were honored and documented.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for DORA compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes vendor and sourcetypes vendor:subproc_notice. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Set `14` to your contractual days; (2) legal owns register updates—Splunk flags drift; (3) exclude immaterial vendors via lookup; (4) integrate CLM tool when available; (5) export for RoI reconciliation. Indexes required: `index=vendor`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=vendor:subproc_notice`.
visualization: Table (lags), Single value (breach count), Timeline. Key fields: `lag_days`, `notice`, `notice_received_at`, `provider_id`, `reg`, `registered_at`.
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Notification lag within contractual window** — Sub-processor notifications arriving within the contractual notice period (typically 30-60 days) are compliant even if flagged.

2. **Entity name changes vs. actual changes** — Corporate restructuring changing sub-processor legal entity names without changing actual processing creates false change signals.

3. **Batch notification processing** — Providers sending consolidated quarterly sub-processor notifications per contract terms may show apparent lag for individual changes.

4. **General authorisation model** — Sub-processor additions under general authorisation (with objection right) follow different notification timelines than specific authorisation.

5. **Contract amendment formalization lag** — Sub-processor changes notified informally may await formal contract amendment execution for full register update.

**Operational suppression:** Maintain a lookup table (`dora_approved_exceptions.csv`) mapping known legitimate activities by process owner, approval reference, and expiry date. Filter the SPL results against this lookup to suppress documented exceptions and reduce alert fatigue. Review and rotate entries quarterly per the ICT risk management framework review cycle.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `vendor` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Lookups**: `roi_subprocessor.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('roi_subprocessor.csv')`.
• **Search-head capacity**: Subsearch-based detections need `subsearch_maxtime` ≥ 60s and `subsearch_maxout` ≥ 50000 in `limits.conf`. For high-volume environments, tune `[search] max_searches_per_cpu` up from default.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.5`. The control objective is: "Evidence that DORA Art.5 (ICT risk-management governance) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.3.32: DORA Outsourcing Registers — Sub-Processor Notification Lag vs Contractual Notice Period." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `vendor` under `vendor:subproc_notice`, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


5 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Set `14` to your contractual days. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 2:** legal owns register updates—Splunk flags drift. The register referenced here is primary evidence — treat changes to it with the same discipline as code changes.

• **Hint 3:** exclude immaterial vendors via lookup. Exclusions should be time-bound and logged — a permanent exclusion baked into the search query becomes invisible over time and future owners cannot tell it was intentional.

• **Hint 4:** integrate CLM tool when available. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 5:** export for RoI reconciliation. Report-generation cadence must match the regulatory reporting cycle; a monthly digest satisfies an annual review requirement but not a quarterly one.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "DORA Outsourcing Registers — Sub-Processor Notification Lag vs Contractual Notice Period," the aggregation produces the result shape required by the DORA control — each row is a summary record the regulation expects to be presentable at audit time.

This search correlates the outer dataset (index=`vendor`, sourcetype=`vendor:subproc_notice`) with a subsearch on `provider_id, subprocessor_name` across the last 400 days. `type=left` preserves all rows from the outer search; nulls in the inner side indicate missing data that may itself constitute a finding.

Join caveat: Splunk's `| join` subsearch is capped at `max_searches_per_cpu * subsearch_maxout` results (default: 50000). If the inner subsearch exceeds this, rows are silently dropped and the resulting report will under-count. Check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd log_level=WARN subsearch` for truncation warnings. For inner searches expected to return >10000 rows, rewrite as `| stats` with a larger base search and post-filter, or use `| tstats` if a CIM datamodel covers the data.

Time-zone sensitivity: the `strptime(notice_received_at, "%Y-%m-%d")` conversion assumes the source timestamp is in the Splunk server's local time zone unless the format includes `%z`. If `notice_received_at` is emitted in UTC by the source system but Splunk's search head runs in a non-UTC zone, the resulting epoch will be off by the zone offset and the subsequent `where _time > notice_received_at_epoch` comparison will produce systematic false positives or negatives. Always validate by comparing `strftime(notice_received_at_epoch, "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M %Z")` against a known-good reference event before promoting to production.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.3.32: DORA Outsourcing Registers — Sub-Processor Notification Lag ]
cron_schedule = 0 */6 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -400d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.3.32,reg=DORA
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=vendor sourcetype=vendor:subproc_notice earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `lag_days`, `notice`, `notice_received_at`, `provider_id`, `reg`, `registered_at`, `subprocessor_name`. Run:

```spl
index=vendor sourcetype=vendor:subproc_notice earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table lag_days, notice, notice_received_at, provider_id, reg, registered_at, subprocessor_name
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(d) Threshold calibration:** Before enabling as a production alert, run the base search with the threshold removed and profile the distribution of `lag_days`:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats perc50(lag_days) as p50 perc90(lag_days) as p90 perc99(lag_days) as p99 max(lag_days) as hi
```

The current threshold `14` should sit between p90 and p99 for a balanced signal-to-noise ratio. If it's below p50, expect heavy alert volume; if above p99, expect under-detection.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.3.32: DORA evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Drill-through table (`lags`):** Full-width bottom row. Enable row-level drilldown to a secondary dashboard that expands each violation with its raw events, timeline, and related context. This is the panel the auditor will actually read — optimise it for scannability (avoid huge wrap-around fields).

**Panel 2 — Single-value KPI (`breach count`):** Place at top-left. Configure `underLabel` with the regulation clause (e.g. 'DORA current violation count') and set a red colour range for any non-zero value. A zero reading is the steady state for a healthy control.

**Panel 3 — Time trend (the key fields):** Place in the main content row. Use `timechart span=1d` aggregated appropriately for the regulation's review cadence (daily for PCI/HIPAA, weekly for ISO-style controls). Annotate the chart with control-change dates so auditors can see when mitigations took effect.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**No results when violations exist:** The most likely cause is subsearch truncation (see Step 2). Inspect `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=searchprocessor log_level=WARN subsearch` for messages like 'Output result count exceeded 50000.'

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**DORA-specific failure modes:**

• Third-party ICT risk register (Art. 28): DORA requires a Register of Information listing every critical ICT third-party service provider. Detections touching supplier risk must reference this register; if a supplier appears in telemetry but not in the Register, that is a Register-completeness finding, not just a technical anomaly.

• Testing cadence (Art. 24-27): DORA's TLPT (Threat-Led Penetration Testing) is required every 3 years for significant institutions. Detections measuring resilience testing must differentiate TLPT (supervisor-led) from self-assessment — mixing them dilutes the evidence.

• Incident classification thresholds (Art. 18): DORA uses specific criteria for 'major' vs 'significant' incidents — client impact, duration, data volume. Detections must classify against these specific thresholds, which differ from GDPR breach criteria; a single event can be major-DORA but not a personal-data breach.

• Resilience testing record retention (Art. 27): results of digital operational resilience tests must be retained. Align log retention to match — typically 5+ years. Verify `frozenTimePeriodInSecs >= 157680000` on any index named by a DORA resilience-testing UC.
grandmaExplanation: We outsourcing arrangements require visibility into sub-processing changes.
